examples.title: Expression and Statement Demonstration
examples.description: Demonstrates various types of expressions and statements in C
examples.explanation: This program shows: 1) Expression statements (arithmetic and assignment), 2) Compound statements using braces to create a block with its own scope, 3) Null statement usage in loops for input validation

examples.title: Operator Precedence Example
examples.description: Demonstrates operator precedence and associativity in expression evaluation
examples.explanation: Demonstrates how operator precedence affects evaluation order. Multiplication has higher precedence than addition, so b*c is computed first. Parentheses can override default precedence. The ++x and y-- operators have side effects.

examples.title: Conditional Operator and Nested Statements
examples.description: Shows ternary operator usage and nested if-else as statements
examples.explanation: Shows the ternary conditional operator (?:) for simple conditions and nested ternary for multiple conditions. Also demonstrates if-else selection statements. The conditional operator provides a compact alternative to if-else for simple value selections.

examples.title: Equivalent Code in Python for Comparison
examples.description: Shows how C expressions and statements translate to Python
examples.code:
# Equivalent Python code for comparison

# Expression statements
a = 10
b = 20
c = a + b  # Assignment expression
print(f"Sum: {c}")

# Compound statement (block) - Python uses indentation
x = 5
print(f"Inside block, x = {x}")

# Conditional operator equivalent in Python
max_val = a if a > b else b
print(f"Maximum of a and b: {max_val}")

# Ternary operator for three values
largest = a if (a > b and a > c) else (b if b > c else c)
print(f"Largest of three: {largest}")

# Python doesn't have switch - uses elif
num = 15
if num % 2 == 0:
    print(f"{num} is Even")
else:
    print(f"{num} is Odd")

# Python's while loop (similar to C)
n = int(input("Enter a number: "))
while n < 0:
    n = int(input("Invalid. Enter again: "))
print(f"You entered: {n}")
examples.explanation: Python doesn't use braces for blocks - indentation defines scope. The ternary operator syntax differs: (true_val) if (condition) else (false_val). Python's walrus operator (:=) introduced in 3.8 allows assignment expressions similar to C.
